actions.thought: I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Although I am in the Small Blind and it only costs 50 more to call, playing this hand out of position against a competent opponent is a losing strategy in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold K8o on the Button (SB) in a Heads-Up match with deep stacks (100BB). K8o is well above the median hand strength in heads-up play. Standard strategy dictates playing a wide range from the button to leverage position. A min-raise to 200 chips is effective here to steal the blind, build the pot when called, and maintain the initiative while keeping the risk low relative to stack depth.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten-Six offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 200, which is a minimum raise. I have already posted 100, so it costs me 100 more to call into a pot that will be 400. This gives me 3-to-1 pot odds. While T6o is a weak hand, in a heads-up situation, it is generally strong enough to defend against a min-raise from the button due to the favorable price, even though I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

I have checked the flop. My hand (Ten-high) missed this board completely, which contains a Jack and a Nine—cards that are likely to be in my opponent's pre-flop raising range. I have no draws and no showdown value currently. Checking is the standard play here to see how the opponent reacts, but I am prepared to fold to most bets.

I hold K8o on a Jd 4c 9d board. After raising pre-flop and getting called, the pot is 400. The opponent checked. This board texture interacts reasonably well with the Big Blind's calling range (flush draws, straight draws, pair of 9s or Js). While I have range advantage, my specific hand is a marginal made hand (K-high) with weak backdoor equity. Betting risks getting check-raised or called by better hands, and I don't fold out many hands that beat me (better K-highs might fold, but pairs won't). Checking back allows me to realize my equity, potentially hit a King or Eight on the turn, and keep the pot size controlled with a medium-strength showdown hand.

I have improved to a pair of sixes on the turn. Since the opponent checked back on the flop, it's less likely they have a strong Jack or a high pocket pair, though they could still have a Nine or a small pair. A pair of sixes with a Ten kicker is a marginal hand with some showdown value. I will check to control the size of the pot and see if the opponent decides to bet. This allows me to evaluate the strength of their hand based on their sizing while avoiding building a large pot out of position with a weak pair.

I hold K8o on a Jd 4c 9d 6h board. The pot is 400. After checking back the flop, I have capped my range, signaling marginal showroom value or air. The opponent has checked to me again on the turn. The 6h is a relatively blank card, though it connects with some straight draws (57, 78). My King-high still has showdown value against the opponent's missed draws (diamonds) and weaker high cards. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff and risks being check-raised, forcing me to fold my equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hit a pair on the river, or induce a bluff on the river from a worse hand. It keeps the pot small, which suits my marginal holding.

On the river, I have improved to two pair with Tens and Sixes. Given the passive action on the flop and turn where both players checked, it is highly likely I have the best hand. A Jack is unlikely in my opponent's range since they checked twice, and while a straight is theoretically possible with 7-8 or Q-8, those hands are less likely to have reached this point in this manner. I will place a value bet of 250 to extract chips from a Nine, a weaker Ten, or a mid-pocket pair.

On the River, the board is Jd 4c 9d 6h Tc. The opponent bets 250 into a pot of 400. I hold Ks 8c, which is just King-high. 

The action went Check-Check on the Flop and Turn, which indicates the opponent likely has a marginal hand, a draw, or a trap. The River Tc is a card that connects with the board, potentially completing straights (Q8, 78) or giving pairs to hands with a Ten. 

I have to call 250 to win 650, requiring about 28% equity. While my 8 blocks some straights (Q8, 87), my hand is extremely weak. I lose to any pair (which the opponent could easily have hit or slow-played), any straight, and even Ace-high missed flush draws. Since many of the natural bluffs (missed diamond draws) might still have Ace-high, my King-high is rarely good here. The risk-reward ratio does not favor a hero call.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 3h 5c, which is a very weak hand. While it is standard to play a wide range of hands from the button in heads-up play, 53 offsuit is near the bottom of the range and is difficult to play profitably post-flop, especially against a competent opponent. I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better spot to engage.

I hold 9h 5s on the button (Small Blind) with 50/100 blinds. This hand is statistically very weak (9-5 offsuit). While I have the advantage of position post-flop, the hand lacks playability due to unconnected ranks and no flush potential. At 100BB deep early in the match, there is no need to loosen my opening range to include the bottom 20% of hands. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here to preserve chips for better spots.

Holding King-Queen offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation is a very strong hand, well above the average starting hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and exert positional pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 4c 2c facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. To call, I need to put in 200 more chips into a pot of 400, offering me 2:1 pot odds. I need roughly 33% equity to break even. While 42s has about 30-34% equity against a wide heads-up opening range, playing this hand Out of Position (OOP) realizes less of that equity due to the difficulty of navigating post-flop streets with such a weak holding. The 3x sizing reduces my pot odds compared to a min-raise, making this a clear fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Q3o (Queen-Three offsuit). In Heads-Up play, the SB acts first pre-flop. While I have position post-flop, Q3o is a very weak hand, ranking in the bottom 35% of hands. It has poor playability and is dominated by many of the opponent's calling hands (e.g., Qx with a better kicker, Kx, Ax). Standard Heads-Up charts often recommend folding Q2o-Q4o. Since the opponent defended their Big Blind in Hand 2 against my raise, I have no reason to believe they are over-folding. Preserving chips by folding this weak hand is the disciplined decision.

98o is a solid hand to open from the button in a heads-up situation. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the big blind and maintain initiative, as I've seen Pro fold to pre-flop aggression in previous hands.

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit (Ah Jd) in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in heads-up play, significantly ahead of the opponent's wide Button opening range. The opponent has raised to 300 (3BB). While a 3-bet for value is a valid option, calling is often superior against a larger open (3x) when Out of Position (OOP) to keep the opponent's range wide and avoid bloating the pot or facing a 4-bet. I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call, and my hand has roughly 60-65% equity against a standard opening range. Therefore, I will call the additional 200 chips to see the flop.

I hold Ah Jd on a flop of 3d 4c 2d. I have two overcards to the board and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5 for the wheel). Although this low board connects somewhat with my calling range, standard strategy Out of Position (OOP) against the pre-flop raiser is to check to them. Leading out (donk betting) is unnecessary and allows the opponent to play perfectly against me. Checking allows me to evaluate their action; if they bet, I have sufficient equity to continue via check-call or potentially check-raise, but for now, the correct play is to check.

I will make a continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot. Although the flop (3d-4c-2d) is low and potentially connects with the Big Blind's defending range, I have the initiative from pre-flop and two overcards. A bet here can often take the pot down immediately if Pro missed the board, and it maintains the aggressive image I've established in previous hands.

On the flop of 3d 4c 2d, facing a half-pot c-bet of 300, I have a strong drawing hand with significant showdown value. Holding Ah Jd, I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5 for the wheel). I am getting 300 to win a pot of 1200 (after my call), offering 25% direct pot odds. My hand has approximately 10 outs (3 Aces, 3 Jacks, 4 Fives) to improve, giving me much better than 25% equity against a wide button c-betting range. Folding is incorrect due to this equity. Raising would likely fold out the opponent's bluffs while isolating me against value hands that beat me. Therefore, calling is the most profitable line to realize my equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn, the board is 3d 4c 2d Ad. I have improved significantly to Top Pair (Aces) with a Jack kicker, and I have picked up a Jack-high flush draw to go with my existing gutshot wheel draw. The Ace is a card that favors the pre-flop raiser's range, but it helps me as well. Leading out (donking) here is generally suboptimal as it folds out the opponent's bluffs and isolates me against better hands (like completed flushes or straights). Checking allows the opponent to continue their aggression with bluffs or value bet worse Ax hands. Given my strong equity and showdown value, I plan to check and call a reasonable bet.

The turn card is the Ace of Diamonds, which is a highly significant card. It completes a possible wheel straight (A-2-3-4-5) and a diamond flush. While the Ace is typically a good card for the pre-flop aggressor to bluff, Pro called my flop bet, indicating they have a piece of this board or a draw. Since I have no showdown value with 9-high and no significant draw, checking back is the most sensible play to control the pot and see a river card for free, rather than turning my hand into a high-variance bluff on a board that likely connected with the opponent's defending range.

On the river, the board is 3d 4c 2d Ad 4d. The 4d puts four diamonds on the board and pairs the fours. I hold Ah Jd, giving me an Ace-high flush (A-J-4-3-2 of diamonds). This is a strong hand, but not the nuts; I lose to the Straight Flush (5d), higher flushes (Kd, Qd), and Full Houses (which are possible given the paired board, though the turn check makes sets/two-pair less likely for the opponent). The opponent checked back the turn, which usually caps their range or suggests a draw/marginal made hand. Leading out here (donk betting) is tricky because worse hands (non-diamond hands) will likely fold, and better hands (boats, nut flushes) will raise. Checking allows me to induce bluffs from missed hands or get value calls from lower flushes if the opponent bets small. Given the board texture is scary (4-flush, paired), the opponent may check back many showdown-value hands, but checking remains the standard line to avoid isolating myself against the top of their range. I intend to check and call a reasonable bet.

I have 9-high with no showdown value on a board that features four diamonds and several straight possibilities. Although Pro has checked, they called my flop continuation bet, which suggests they have some connection to this board. Given the Ace of Diamonds on the turn and the fourth diamond on the river, it is highly likely Pro holds at least a pair or a small diamond. Bluffing into a range that called the flop on such a wet board is risky, as even a weak diamond will likely call. Checking back is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack and move to the next hand.

I hold Pocket Sevens (7d 7s) on the Button (Small Blind). In Heads-Up play, this is a top 6-7% hand and clearly strong enough to open for value. I have position post-flop. A min-raise to 200 is a standard line to build the pot while keeping the opponent's range wide, though I am comfortable calling a 3-bet given the stack depth (100bb+). Folding is incorrect due to hand strength, and limping allows the Big Blind to realize equity for free, which I want to avoid.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit. The opponent has raised to 200 (a 2x open). While the pot odds are 3:1 to call, Ten-Trey offsuit is one of the weakest hands in the range and performs poorly out of position. Given the early stage of the match and the lack of playability for this hand, I will choose to fold and wait for better spots rather than defending with junk.

I am in the Small Blind with 7d 3h, which is an extremely weak starting hand. While I am on the button and could technically attempt a steal or complete the blind, 7-3 offsuit has very little post-flop playability and equity. Given the tournament context and the strength of the hand, folding is the most disciplined and standard play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I hold 9d 2s (92o) on the Button/SB. This is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in heads-up Texas Hold'em. While I have position post-flop, the equity of this hand is extremely low, and it has poor playability. My opponent has shown some capability to raise or call, so trying to steal the blind with bottom-tier trash carries unnecessary negative EV. The standard and most profitable play here is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with T3s, which is a marginal but playable hand in heads-up poker, especially since it is suited. Pro has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop raises in previous hands (Hand #5). I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately. If called, the suited nature of the hand gives me some post-flop equity.

I hold 4h 9c in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (to 300) from the Button. My hand is very weak (9-4 offsuit), having poor playability and low equity against even a wide opening range. Although I'm getting 2:1 pot odds (need 33% equity), realizing that equity out of position with such a weak hand is difficult. With a 3x raise making the price steeper than a min-raise, and having one of the worst hands in the deck, folding is the standard and correct decision.

I hold Ace-Nine offsuit (Ah 9d) on the Button/Small Blind. In heads-up poker, an Ace is a very strong hand, well above the median starting hand strength. Being in position post-flop adds significant value. My opponent (Flash) has folded to my pre-flop raises in the past (e.g., Hand #8). Raising is the standard play here to build the pot with a good hand, take the initiative, and potentially take down the blinds immediately. A minimum raise to 200 is a balanced size that risks little while asserting strength.

I have a pocket pair (22) in the Big Blind. In heads-up play, any pocket pair has significant value. The Small Blind (Pro) has min-raised to 200, and I need to commit 100 more to see the flop, giving me excellent pot odds of 3:1. While 22 is vulnerable to overcards, it is often ahead of a wide button-opening range. I will call the raise to keep the pot size controlled while playing out of position, looking to either hit a set or reach a cheap showdown on favorable board textures.

I have a small pocket pair on a paired board (5-5-7). While I have two pair (5s and 2s), any 7 or higher pocket pair beats me, and any 5 has trips. As the Big Blind acting first post-flop, I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their reaction and control the pot size with my marginal holding.

I hold Ace-Nine offsuit on a paired board of [5d 7c 5c]. The pot is 400. Flash checked to me. While I have the lead and likely the best hand with Ace-high, the board texture interacts well with the Big Blind's calling range (potential 5x, 7x, or draws). Betting has merits to deny equity to random overcards, but checking back is a strong option for pot control. It allows me to realize my equity, keeps the pot small for a marginal made hand, avoids getting check-raised, and potentially induces bluffs on later streets. Checking is a low-variance play that preserves my stack while maintaining a chance to win at showdown.

On this 5d-7c-5c-9h board, my pocket deuces are very weak. The flop went check-check, suggesting Pro might have overcards or a marginal hand he's trying to take to showdown. The 9h on the turn is another overcard to my pair. I will check again to control the pot and try to reach a cheap showdown, as betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me.

I checked back the flop with two overcards for pot control. The turn brings a Nine, giving me Top Pair, Top Kicker (A9) on a board of [5d 7c 5c 9h]. My opponent checked to me again. Given my hand strength has improved significantly, I should now bet to extract value from worse pairs (like 7x or pocket pairs 66/88) and charge flush/straight draws. Checking back would give a free card to draws and miss value. A half-pot bet of 200 is appropriate to balance value and protection.

Pro checked the flop but is now betting 200 into a 400 pot on the turn after a 9h appeared. My pocket deuces are the absolute bottom of my range here. On this board (5-7-5-9), I am losing to any 5, 7, or 9, as well as any pocket pair higher than 22. Even if Pro is bluffing with overcards like KQ or JT, they have many outs to beat me on the river. I have no reason to believe my hand is good enough to call a bet out of position, and I would be in a difficult spot on almost any river card. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

In heads-up play, Queen-Seven offsuit is a strong enough hand to open from the Button/Small Blind. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that allows me to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the opponent in the Big Blind, while also maintaining position for the rest of the hand if they call.

Opponent has opened to 300 (3bb) from the button, consistent with their previous aggressive sizing. AQs is a premium hand that dominates the opponent's wide button opening range. While calling is a valid low-variance option, 3-betting is superior here to punish the larger open size, extract value from worse hands (like weaker Ax, Kx, Qx), and take the initiative while out of position. A raise to 1100 (approx 3.7x the open) puts maximum pressure on the opponent and builds a pot with a significant equity advantage.

I opened with Q7o from the button, which is a standard play, but my opponent has responded with a significant 3-bet to 1100. Queen-Seven offsuit does not play well against a 3-betting range, even with the advantage of position post-flop. Calling 800 more would commit a significant portion of my stack to a pot where I am likely dominated by hands like AQ, KQ, or pocket pairs. Given that we are still early in the match and I have a healthy stack, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with Ten-Three offsuit (T3o). In heads-up play, the Button typically opens a wide range of hands because they will have position post-flop. However, T3o is at the very bottom of the starting hand distribution (approx. bottom 15%). It lacks connectivity, suitedness, and high card strength. Even with position, it is difficult to play profitably against a competent opponent. While I have been aggressive in previous hands, opening this specific hand is likely negative EV. Folding is the standard and prudent play here.

I am on the button with Qd7d, a suited hand that has decent playability in a heads-up situation. My stack is 9200 chips, which is roughly 92 big blinds. Raising to 300 (a 3x open) has been my standard size in previous hands. This allows me to take control of the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop, or play a pot in position if called.

I hold Pocket Sevens (7c 7h) in the Big Blind. The opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3bb) from the Button. In heads-up play, 77 is a strong hand, well ahead of a wide button opening range. While a 3-bet is a viable option to deny equity or take the pot down immediately, calling is the standard play here. It keeps the pot manageable while playing out of position and keeps the opponent's range wide, including hands I dominate. I am getting good pot odds to call and see a flop.

I hold Pocket Sevens (7c 7h) on a paired flop of 4h 5h 5d. I have an overpair to the 4, which is a strong hand heads-up, but I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. The board texture is somewhat wet with flush and straight draws present. Standard strategy suggests checking to the raiser to allow them to make a continuation bet with a wide range, including bluffs and draws. Leading out (donking) would likely fold out their weaker hands that I want to keep in the pot or isolate me against better hands like a 5 or higher overpair. Therefore, checking is the most prudent play to keep their range wide and evaluate their action.

On this 4h5h5d flop, I have Queen-high with a backdoor flush draw. The board is paired, which generally favors the pre-flop aggressor as it is harder for the big blind to have connected strongly. My opponent checked to me, which is expected. A continuation bet here is effective because it can force folds from hands that missed entirely or from weak overcards. I'll bet roughly half the pot to maintain pressure and potentially take it down now.

I hold Pocket Sevens on a paired board of 4h 5h 5d. My opponent has continuation bet 300 (half pot) after I checked. My hand is currently strong, beating all of the opponent's unconnected overcards and straight/flush draws, but vulnerable to overcards coming on the turn. Raising here likely folds out the bluffs I want to keep in the pot (like A-high or K-high) and isolates me against better hands (like a 5 or higher pairs). Calling allows me to control the pot size while out of position and evaluate the turn card. The price is good to continue.

On the turn, the King of Diamonds appears, making the board 4h 5h 5d Kd. This is an unfavorable card for my pocket Sevens as it connects well with the opponent's range (Kx hands usually open and c-bet). I am now losing to any King, any 5, and higher pocket pairs. However, I am still ahead of flush draws (hearts and diamonds), straight draws, and pure bluffs. Leading out here serves little purpose as better hands will call and worse hands will fold. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see if the opponent decides to barrel on this scare card. If they check back, I likely have the best hand heading to the river.

The turn King of Diamonds is an excellent card for my range as the pre-flop aggressor, and it also gives me a diamond flush draw to go with my Queen high. Since Pro checked again, I will fire a second barrel. This bet puts significant pressure on Pro's marginal holdings like a 4, a small pocket pair, or heart draws that may now be incentivized to fold. Even if called, I have 9 outs to a flush and potentially 3 outs to a winning Queen on the river.

On the turn, the King of Diamonds is a significant scare card that connects well with the opponent's button opening and continuation betting range. While I was ahead of many hands on the flop, the arrival of the King means I am now losing to all Kx combinations, as well as any 5x or higher pocket pairs. The opponent's bet of 700 (over half pot) signals strength or a strong semi-bluff. My hand (77) is effectively a bluff-catcher now, drawing to only two outs if behind. Given the board texture with multiple draws (two flush draws) and the range advantage shifting to the aggressor, continuing out of position is marginal at best. Preserving the stack is preferable to calling down with a weak underpair in a spot where the opponent is credible.

In Heads-Up play, the button should open widely (often 70-80% of hands). I previously raised with Q7o (Hand #1) and won the pot immediately. J7o is similar in strength and playability. My opponent (Flash) has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop aggression (Hands #1, #3, #8, #9, #13), and I have been folding my button too frequently recently (folded 4 of the last 7 buttons). To balance my range and capitalize on the opponent's fold frequency, I will min-raise to steal the blind. I have position post-flop if called.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 2s. Pro has raised from the Small Blind to 200, making it 100 more for me to call. 9s 2s is a weak hand, even in a heads-up situation. While the price to call is attractive (3:1), this hand has very poor playability post-flop and often gets into trouble. Given the stack depths and the fact that I want to minimize losses with marginal hands, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with 9h4h. This is a suited hand that, while not premium, is strong enough to open in a heads-up or small-table scenario to maintain aggression and potentially steal the blinds. I have been raising frequently from this position to keep the opponent off-balance. A standard 3x raise to 300 chips is consistent with my previous play.

The opponent opens to 300 (3BB) from the button, a standard sizing they have used consistently. Heads-up with 100BB effective stacks, 7hTh (T7s) is a defendable hand due to its playability (flush and straight potential). Although I will be out of position post-flop, the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600, requiring 33% equity) are favorable enough given T7s has approximately 35-40% equity against a wide button opening range. Folding is too tight, and 3-betting is unnecessary. I will call to see a flop.

I hold middle pair (Sevens) on a Q-7-2 board with a flush draw present. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard line is to check to the aggressor. My hand has showdown value but is not strong enough to lead (donk bet) for value, as that would likely only get action from better hands (Queens, overpairs) or strong draws, while folding out the bluffs I want to keep in the range. Checking allows me to potentially call a c-bet or see a free turn to improve my hand.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with 9h4h. The flop of Qc 2d 7c is relatively dry, although it does contain a club draw. Since the Big Blind checked to me, a continuation bet is the standard play to represent a strong hand like a Queen or a pocket pair. I will bet 300 chips, which is half the pot, to maintain my table image of aggression and attempt to take the pot down right now.

I hold middle pair (Sevens) with a Ten kicker on a Q-7-2 board. The opponent, who raised pre-flop from the button, has continued with a half-pot bet of 300. Given the wide range of a button opener, my hand is ahead of many semi-bluffs (flush draws, straight draws like J9) and pure bluffs (air). While I am behind top pair (Queens) or better pocket pairs, folding middle pair to a single standard continuation bet is too tight and exploitable in a heads-up match. The price to call is 300 to win a pot that will be 1200, requiring 25% equity, which I certainly have against a wide range. Raising would isolate me against better hands, so calling is the most prudent play to keep the opponent's bluffing range wide and re-evaluate on the turn.

I hold middle pair (Sevens) on a Q-8-7-2 board. The turn card (8h) is an overcard to my pair but keeps me with second pair. Out of position against the pre-flop aggressor who c-bet the flop, my hand acts as a bluff-catcher. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called by better hands (Queens, 8x, overpairs) and fold out the bluffs I am currently beating. Checking allows me to pot-control and potentially induce further bluffs or get to a cheaper showdown. I will check to the raiser.

I am currently holding 9-high with no significant draws on a board of Qc 2d 7c 8h. I was the pre-flop and flop aggressor. The opponent called my flop continuation bet, which suggests they likely have a piece of the board, such as a Queen, a 7, or a club draw. The 8h turn doesn't change much, and since the opponent checked to me again, I have the option to check back to see a free river card. Betting a second barrel here with total air against a range that called the flop is risky, especially as the opponent has shown a willingness to fold to turn pressure before, but also a willingness to stick around with pairs. I will check to keep the pot manageable and see how the river develops.

I hold a pair of Sevens on a board of Qc-2d-7c-8h-Kc. The river card is bad for my hand, bringing an overcard (King) and completing the club flush draw. My opponent checked back the turn, capping their range, but that range likely contained flush draws or King-high hands which have now improved. Betting here has little utility: worse hands (missed draws, lower pairs) will fold, and better hands (Kings, Queens, Eights, Flushes) will call or raise. Checking allows me to try to get to showdown cheaply against missed straight draws or lower pairs. If the opponent bets, I will likely have to fold given the dangerous board texture.

I have 9-high with absolutely no showdown value on a board of Qc 2d 7c 8h Kc. I was the pre-flop and flop aggressor, but I checked the turn. The river King of clubs is a significant card because it completes a potential flush and is an overcard to the Queen. Pro has checked three times (calling only on the flop), which strongly suggests a weak made hand like a 7 or a 2, or a missed draw. Since I cannot win this pot by checking, I will attempt a bluff. A bet of 800 (two-thirds of the pot) represents a King or a completed flush, putting significant pressure on Pro's marginal holdings and likely forcing a fold.

On the river, the board is Qc-2d-7c-8h-Kc. I hold a pair of Sevens (3rd/4th pair) with no club. The river card is very bad for my hand: it completes the club flush draw and brings a King, which hits many hands in the opponent's range that might have checked back the turn (such as K-high or flush draws). The opponent bets 800 into 1200 (2/3 pot). My hand only beats pure bluffs. Given that the runout favors the opponent's range (improving draws and high-card floats) and I lose to all value hands including Qx, Kx, 8x, and flushes, my hand is too weak to bluff-catch. I have better hands in my range to defend with (like Qx or hands with a club blocker). Folding is the prudent play.

I hold 92 offsuit on the button (SB), which is statistically one of the weakest hands in heads-up poker. Despite having the positional advantage post-flop, the hand has very poor playability and equity. Early in the match with deep stacks (almost 100 BB), there is no incentive to force action with such a weak holding. I have established a pattern of folding the bottom of my range in this position (e.g., Hand 10 with 92o, Hand 4 with 95o), so folding here maintains consistency and avoids unnecessary chip loss.

I am holding Deuce of Hearts and Six of Diamonds on the Button. This is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. While heads-up strategy allows for a wide range of opening hands from the small blind, 62o is generally too weak to play profitably against a competent opponent. I have shown a willingness to fold weak hands from this position in previous rounds (Hands 1, 3, 9), and I will continue to maintain a disciplined range by folding this hand.

I hold 8d7d (suited connectors) on the Button (Small Blind). This is a strong speculative hand in heads-up play, with good playability post-flop due to straight and flush potential. Given the stack depth (96 BB) and position, raising is superior to limping or folding.
